Further Dissensions among the Arians at Constantinople. The Psathyrians.

ccel.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

title: Further Dissensions among the Arians at Constantinople. The Psathyrians.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But dissensions arose among the Arians also on this account.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The contentious questions which were daily agitated among them, led them to start the most absurd propositions.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For whereas it has been always believed in the church that God is the Father of the Son, the Word, it was asked whether God could be called ‘Father’ before the Son had subsistence?

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Thus in asserting that the Word of God was not begotten of the Father, but was created out ‘of nothing,’ and thus falling into error on the chief and main point, they deservedly fell into absurd cavilings about a mere name.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Dorotheus therefore being sent for by them from Antioch maintained that God neither was nor could be called Father before the Son existed.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But Marinus whom they had summoned out of Thrace before Dorotheus, piqued at the superior deference which was paid to his rival, undertook to defend the contrary opinion.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: In consequence of these things there arose a schism among them, and being thus divided respecting this term, each party held separate meetings.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Those under Dorotheus retained their original places of assembly: but the followers of Marinus built distinct oratories for themselves, and asserted that the Father had always been Father, even when the Son was not.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This section of the Arians was denominated Psathyrians, because one of the most zealous defenders of this opinion was Theoctistus, a Syrian by birth, and a cake-seller [Psathyropola] by trade.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Selenas bishop of the Goths adopted the views of this party, a man of mixed descent; he was a Goth by his father’s side, but by his mother’s a Phrygian, by which means he taught in the church with great readiness in both these languages.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This faction however soon quarreled among themselves, Marinus disagreeing with Agapius, whom he himself had preferred to the bishopric of Ephesus.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: They disputed, however, not about any point of religion, but in narrow-mindedness about precedence, in which the Goths sided with Agapius.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Wherefore many of the ecclesiastics under their jurisdiction, abominating the vain-glorious contest between these two, abandoned them both, and became adherents to the ‘homoousian’ faith.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The Arians having continued thus divided among themselves during the space of thirty-five years, were reunited in the reign of Theodosius the Younger, under the consulate of Plintha the commander-in-chief of the army, he being a member of the sect of Psathyrians; these were prevailed on to desist from contention.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: They afterwards passed a resolution, giving it all the cogency of law, that the question which had led to their separation, should never be mooted again.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But this reconciliation extended no farther than Constantinople; for in other cities where any of these two parties were found, they persisted in their former separation.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: So much respecting the division among the Arians.
